Slide title: Embedded Firmware Before an RTOS
Teaching focus: Explain bare-metal firmware organized around initialization, super-loop polling, interrupt service routines, drivers, and shared state.
Visual plan: Embedded firmware architecture diagram: boot/init, super loop, ISR events, peripheral drivers, shared flags, hardware boundary.
Required visible text: Bare-metal firmware often starts with a loop and interrupts.

Slide title: When a Loop Stops Scaling
Teaching focus: Show why a simple loop becomes fragile as timing constraints, multiple peripherals, blocking operations, and state interactions grow.
Visual plan: Conceptual pressure diagram where a central loop is pulled by UART, sensor, display, storage, timing, and control tasks.
Required visible text: A loop can work until timing and responsibility boundaries become unclear.

Slide title: Real-Time Means Predictable, Not Fast
Teaching focus: Define latency, jitter, deadline, period, response time, and determinism for embedded systems.
Visual plan: Timing diagram with event, response, deadline, jitter band, period markers, and predictable behavior emphasis.
Required visible text: Real-time means bounded response, not maximum speed.

Slide title: Multitasking on a Single Core
Teaching focus: Introduce the idea that multiple tasks can share one CPU through rapid context switches and scheduler decisions.
Visual plan: Single CPU core timeline with Task A, Task B, Task C alternating execution, register save/restore hint, no clutter.
Required visible text: Multitasking is structured sharing of one processor.

Slide title: What the Scheduler Decides
Teaching focus: Explain Ready, Running, Blocked, and Suspended states and how the scheduler chooses CPU ownership.
Visual plan: Task state machine with Ready queue, Running CPU, Blocked wait points, Suspended state, arrows for scheduling decisions.
Required visible text: The scheduler chooses among tasks that are ready to run.

Slide title: What FreeRTOS Is
Teaching focus: Position FreeRTOS as a small RTOS kernel linked into firmware, not a separate desktop-style operating system.
Visual plan: Layered firmware image: application tasks, FreeRTOS kernel, portable layer, MCU hardware, FreeRTOSConfig.h controls behavior.
Required visible text: FreeRTOS is linked into the firmware image.

Slide title: Why Use an RTOS?
Teaching focus: Explain engineering value: blocking without busy waiting, separate responsibilities, deterministic handoff, portable kernel mechanisms, and diagnostics.
Visual plan: Value map with five spokes: timing, structure, communication, synchronization, diagnostics, centered on RTOS kernel.
Required visible text: An RTOS makes execution structure explicit.

Slide title: Event-Driven Firmware
Teaching focus: Introduce event-driven design: tasks wait for events instead of polling continuously.
Visual plan: Flow diagram from hardware event to ISR or driver to queue/notification to task wake-up and processing.
Required visible text: Waiting is a design tool, not wasted time.

Slide title: Interrupts Stay Short
Teaching focus: Explain deferred interrupt processing: ISR handles urgent work and wakes a task for heavier processing.
Visual plan: ISR-to-task handoff infographic with short ISR, FromISR API, context switch decision, high-priority worker task.
Required visible text: Interrupt handlers should trigger work, not do all work.
